nodes.label: Marxism
nodes.description: A method of socioeconomic analysis that uses a materialist interpretation of historical development to understand class relations and social conflict, as well as a dialectical perspective to view social transformation. Developed by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels in the mid-19th century.

nodes.label: Anarchism
nodes.description: A political philosophy that advocates self-governed societies based on voluntary institutions. These are often described as stateless societies, but several authors have defined them more specifically as institutions based on non-hierarchical, free associations.

nodes.label: Democratic Socialism
nodes.description: A political philosophy that advocates political democracy alongside social ownership of the means of production, often with an emphasis on self-management and democratic management of economic institutions.

nodes.label: Socialist Feminism
nodes.description: A branch of feminism that focuses upon both the public and private spheres of a woman's life and argues that liberation can only be achieved by working to end both the economic and cultural sources of women's oppression.

nodes.label: Anti-Colonialism
nodes.description: A political movement opposing colonial rule. Historically, anti-colonialism encompassed resistance to imperial power, and it has developed into a broader critique of capitalism, racism, and global inequality.

nodes.label: Syndicalism
nodes.description: A radical current within the labour movement that advocates taking control of the economy through industrial unions. Syndicalists consider trade unions to be the potential means of achieving a libertarian socialist economy.

nodes.label: Trotskyism
nodes.description: The theory of Marxism as advocated by Leon Trotsky. Trotsky identified as an orthodox Marxist and Bolshevik-Leninist and supported founding a vanguard party of the working class to lead a socialist revolution.

nodes.label: Council Communism
nodes.description: A current of communist thought that emerged in the 1920s, primarily in Germany and the Netherlands. It opposes the idea of a vanguard party and emphasizes workers' councils as the basis for a socialist society.

nodes.label: Libertarian Socialism
nodes.description: A group of political philosophies that emphasize the anti-authoritarian aspects of socialism. It advocates for decentralized, non-hierarchical forms of organization in economic and political life.

nodes.label: Critical Theory
nodes.description: A social philosophy associated with the Frankfurt School that critiques and seeks to remedy the ideological dimensions of contemporary capitalism. It draws on Marxism, psychoanalysis, and other traditions to analyze culture and society.

nodes.label: Frankfurt School
nodes.description: A school of social theory and critical philosophy associated with the Institute for Social Research at Goethe University Frankfurt. Key figures include Adorno, Horkheimer, Marcuse, and Benjamin, who developed Critical Theory.

nodes.label: Situationism
nodes.description: An international organization of social revolutionaries made up of avant-garde artists and political theorists, active from 1957 to 1972. Their critique of the spectacle — the dominance of commodity culture — was hugely influential.

nodes.label: Eco-Socialism
nodes.description: A political ideology merging aspects of socialism with that of green politics, ecology and alter-globalization or anti-globalization. It argues that the root cause of environmental destruction is capitalism, not just industrial modernity.

nodes.label: Black Radical Tradition
nodes.description: A body of thought that critiques racial capitalism and the specific forms of oppression faced by Black people under capitalism. Associated with thinkers like C.L.R. James, W.E.B. Du Bois, and Cedric Robinson.

nodes.label: Autonomism
nodes.description: A set of left-wing political and social movements and theories closely related to the labor movement that emerged in Italy in the 1960s. It developed a theory of the social factory and constituent power.

nodes.label: Leninism
nodes.description: The political ideology and practical application of Marxism developed by Vladimir Lenin, including the theory of vanguardism — that the working class needs a revolutionary party to lead it to power.

nodes.label: Postcolonial Theory
nodes.description: An academic discipline that analyzes, explains, and responds to the cultural legacy of colonialism and imperialism, focusing on the human consequences of the control and exploitation of colonized people.

nodes.label: Western Marxism
nodes.description: A current of Marxist thought in the Western world that emphasized the superstructure — culture, ideology, and consciousness — as much as the economic base. Associated with Gramsci, Lukács, and the Frankfurt School.

nodes.label: Market Socialism
nodes.description: A type of economic system involving the public, cooperative, or social ownership of the means of production within the framework of a market economy.

nodes.label: Mutualism
nodes.description: An anarchist school of thought and economic theory that advocates a society where each person possesses a means of production, either individually or collectively, with trade representing equivalent amounts of labor in the free market.

nodes.label: Left Accelerationism
nodes.description: A political tendency arguing that the contradictions of capitalism should be accelerated rather than resisted, with the goal of creating conditions for a post-capitalist future. Nick Srnicek and Alex Williams are key figures.

nodes.label: Post-Capitalism
nodes.description: The idea that capitalism is either actively being, or could be, replaced by a new economic system. Associated with thinkers like Paul Mason and Mark Fisher who saw digital technology and the 2008 crisis as opening new possibilities.

nodes.label: Hegemony Theory
nodes.description: Developed by Antonio Gramsci, this theory explains how dominant classes maintain control not just through coercion but through cultural and ideological consent — the organization of consensus in civil society.

nodes.label: Dialectical Materialism
nodes.description: The philosophy of Marxism, which combines a materialist ontology — the primacy of the physical world — with a dialectical method of analysis, seeing history as driven by contradictions and their resolution.

nodes.label: Theory of Alienation
nodes.description: Marx's concept describing the estrangement of people from aspects of their human nature as a consequence of living and working in a society stratified into social classes. Workers are alienated from their product, their labor, their species-being.

nodes.label: Karl Marx
nodes.description: German philosopher, economist, historian, sociologist, political theorist, journalist, and socialist revolutionary. Author of The Communist Manifesto and Capital. Developed historical materialism, the labor theory of value, and the theory of surplus value.

nodes.label: Friedrich Engels
nodes.description: German philosopher, historian, political scientist, and socialist. Alongside Marx, co-wrote The Communist Manifesto and edited the second and third volumes of Capital. Contributed to the theory of dialectical materialism.

nodes.label: Antonio Gramsci
nodes.description: Italian Marxist philosopher and communist politician. Wrote the Prison Notebooks while imprisoned by Mussolini's regime. Developed influential theories of cultural hegemony, the organic intellectual, and the war of position.

nodes.label: Rosa Luxemburg
nodes.description: Polish-born German revolutionary socialist, Marxist philosopher, and anti-war activist. Co-founded the Spartacist League and the Communist Party of Germany. Critiqued Leninism and developed spontaneism theory.

nodes.label: Leon Trotsky
nodes.description: Ukrainian-born Russian Marxist revolutionary and theorist. Developed the theory of permanent revolution. Led the Red Army and later founded the Fourth International in opposition to Stalinism.

nodes.label: Mikhail Bakunin
nodes.description: Russian revolutionary anarchist and founder of collectivist anarchism. A major figure in the First International who clashed with Marx over the role of the state. Advocated the immediate abolition of the state.

nodes.label: Peter Kropotkin
nodes.description: Russian anarchist communist and scientist. Developed the theory of mutual aid as an evolutionary principle opposing Social Darwinism. Wrote The Conquest of Bread, advocating decentralized, free communism.

nodes.label: Emma Goldman
nodes.description: Lithuanian-American anarchist political activist and writer. A prominent figure in the development of anarchist political philosophy in North America and Europe. Advocated for free love, women's liberation, and workers' rights.

nodes.label: Frantz Fanon
nodes.description: Martinican-Algerian psychiatrist, philosopher, and political theorist. Wrote The Wretched of the Earth, a seminal text on decolonization, violence, and the psychology of the colonized. Hugely influential in anti-colonial thought.

nodes.label: C.L.R. James
nodes.description: Trinidadian historian, journalist, and Marxist theorist. Wrote The Black Jacobins, a history of the Haitian Revolution. Developed an analysis of the intersection of race and class outside Eurocentric Marxism.

nodes.label: W.E.B. Du Bois
nodes.description: American sociologist, historian, civil rights activist, Pan-Africanist, and socialist. Wrote The Souls of Black Folk and Black Reconstruction. Developed the concept of double consciousness and the color line.

nodes.label: Theodor Adorno
nodes.description: German philosopher, sociologist, psychologist, musicologist, and composer. A leading member of the Frankfurt School. Co-wrote Dialectic of Enlightenment with Horkheimer, critiquing the culture industry and Enlightenment reason.

nodes.label: Max Horkheimer
nodes.description: German philosopher and sociologist, director of the Frankfurt School's Institute for Social Research. Developed critical theory and co-authored Dialectic of Enlightenment, arguing that Enlightenment rationality becomes domination.

nodes.label: Guy Debord
nodes.description: French theorist, filmmaker, member of the Letterist International, and founding member of the Situationist International. Wrote The Society of the Spectacle, arguing that authentic social life had been replaced with its representation.

nodes.label: Herbert Marcuse
nodes.description: German-American philosopher, political theorist, and sociologist. Associated with the Frankfurt School. Wrote One-Dimensional Man, arguing that advanced industrial society was repressively tolerant. Hugely influential in the New Left.

nodes.label: Murray Bookchin
nodes.description: American libertarian socialist, political philosopher, and ecologist. Founded the political theory of social ecology and libertarian municipalism. Influenced the Kurdish freedom movement through his idea of democratic confederalism.

nodes.label: Antonio Negri
nodes.description: Italian Marxist sociologist and political philosopher. A founding figure of Autonomism. Co-wrote Empire and Multitude with Michael Hardt, analyzing post-Fordist capitalism and the emerging form of sovereignty.

nodes.label: David Harvey
nodes.description: British Marxist economic geographer and social theorist. Wrote The Limits to Capital and A Brief History of Neoliberalism. Known for his concept of accumulation by dispossession and spatial theories of capitalism.

nodes.label: Slavoj Žižek
nodes.description: Slovenian philosopher, cultural critic, and Hegelian Marxist. Senior Researcher at the Institute for Sociology and Philosophy in Ljubljana. Engages Lacanian psychoanalysis, Hegel, and Marx to critique ideology and contemporary capitalism.

nodes.label: Mark Fisher
nodes.description: British theorist, critic, and philosopher. Wrote Capitalist Realism, coining the term to describe the widespread sense that capitalism is easier to imagine than any alternative. Also wrote Ghosts of My Life on hauntology.

nodes.label: Simone de Beauvoir
nodes.description: French existentialist philosopher, writer, social theorist, and feminist activist. Wrote The Second Sex, a foundational text in feminist philosophy. Argued that women are not born but become women through social construction.

nodes.label: bell hooks
nodes.description: American author, professor, feminist, and social activist. Wrote Feminism Is for Everybody and Ain't I a Woman?. Developed an intersectional analysis of gender, race, and class, arguing for a feminist politics of love.

nodes.label: Louis Althusser
nodes.description: French Marxist philosopher and public intellectual. Developed structuralist Marxism, arguing for the relative autonomy of ideology. Introduced the concepts of ideological state apparatuses and interpellation.

nodes.label: György Lukács
nodes.description: Hungarian Marxist philosopher and literary critic. Wrote History and Class Consciousness, reintroducing the concept of reification and developing the notion of totality as central to Marxist method.

nodes.label: Walter Benjamin
nodes.description: German Jewish philosopher, cultural critic, and essayist. Associated with the Frankfurt School. Wrote The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction and Theses on the Philosophy of History, on redemption and historical materialism.

nodes.label: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon
nodes.description: French philosopher, mutualist economist, and socialist. Famously declared 'Property is theft!' in What is Property? The first prominent thinker to call himself an anarchist.

nodes.label: Cedric Robinson
nodes.description: American political scholar who coined the term 'racial capitalism' in Black Marxism. Argued that capitalism was born of and remains dependent on racial differentiation and the super-exploitation of racialized labor.

nodes.label: Angela Davis
nodes.description: American political activist, philosopher, and author. Member of the Communist Party USA and Black Panther Party. Wrote Are Prisons Obsolete?, founding the prison abolition movement, and Women, Race and Class.

nodes.label: Jean-Paul Sartre
nodes.description: French existentialist philosopher and committed Marxist. Wrote Critique of Dialectical Reason, attempting to reconcile existentialism and Marxism. Supported anti-colonial struggles and critiqued the Soviet Union.

nodes.label: Clara Zetkin
nodes.description: German Marxist theorist and activist in both the Social Democratic Party and the Communist Party of Germany. Founder of International Women's Day. Developed a socialist feminist analysis of women's oppression.

nodes.label: Errico Malatesta
nodes.description: Italian anarchist communist. One of the most influential anarchist thinkers of the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Advocated insurrectionary anarchism and worked alongside Bakunin and Kropotkin.

nodes.label: Paul Mason
nodes.description: British journalist and author. Wrote PostCapitalism: A Guide to Our Future, arguing that information technology is dissolving capitalism from within and making possible a new form of post-capitalist economy.

nodes.label: Vladimir Lenin
nodes.description: Russian revolutionary, politician, and political theorist. Leader of the Bolshevik Party and architect of the October Revolution of 1917. Developed vanguard party theory, the concept of imperialism as the highest stage of capitalism, and the theory of the revolutionary state in The State and Revolution.

nodes.label: Edward Said
nodes.description: Palestinian-American literary theorist, cultural critic, and public intellectual. Professor at Columbia University. Wrote Orientalism (1978), founding the field of postcolonial studies by demonstrating how Western scholarship constructed the 'Orient' as inferior and exotic to legitimize colonial domination.

nodes.label: Capital, Vol. I
nodes.description: Marx's critique of political economy. Analyzes the capitalist mode of production through the commodity, surplus value, exploitation, and the general law of capitalist accumulation.

nodes.label: The Communist Manifesto
nodes.description: Written by Marx and Engels for the Communist League. Presents an analytical approach to the class struggle and the problems of capitalism, arguing that capitalism will be overthrown by the proletariat.

nodes.label: Prison Notebooks
nodes.description: Gramsci's collection of notes and essays written while imprisoned. Introduces concepts of cultural hegemony, the organic intellectual, passive revolution, and the war of position vs. war of maneuver.

nodes.label: The State and Revolution
nodes.description: Lenin's analysis of the role of the state in capitalist society and during the transition to communism. Argues that the bourgeois state must be smashed rather than seized.

nodes.label: The Mass Strike
nodes.description: Luxemburg's analysis of the 1905 Russian Revolution and the political dynamics of the mass strike as a form of political struggle. Critiqued trade union reformism and Leninist vanguardism.

nodes.label: The Society of the Spectacle
nodes.description: Debord's central theoretical work. Argues that authentic social life has been replaced with its representation — the spectacle — and that the commodity form has colonized all aspects of life.

nodes.label: One-Dimensional Man
nodes.description: Marcuse's analysis of advanced industrial society. Argues that it produces a 'one-dimensional' universe of thought and behavior in which the capacity for critical negation is absorbed and neutralized.

nodes.label: The Wretched of the Earth
nodes.description: Fanon's analysis of decolonization and the psychopathology of colonization. Argues that violence is a necessary part of the decolonization process and analyzes the pitfalls of national consciousness.

nodes.label: Black Reconstruction in America
nodes.description: Du Bois's history of the Reconstruction Era, arguing that Black workers were central agents in the Civil War and Reconstruction. Challenged white supremacist historiography.

nodes.label: Feminism Is for Everybody
nodes.description: bell hooks's accessible introduction to feminist politics, arguing that feminism must be a mass movement that addresses race and class alongside gender.

nodes.label: The Second Sex
nodes.description: Beauvoir's foundational feminist text. Analyzes the treatment and perception of women throughout history. Famous for the claim: 'One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.'

nodes.label: Dialectic of Enlightenment
nodes.description: Adorno and Horkheimer's analysis of why the Enlightenment, which promised human liberation through reason, gave rise to fascism and mass culture instead. Introduces the culture industry thesis.

nodes.label: History and Class Consciousness
nodes.description: Lukács's collection of essays that reintroduced Hegel to Marxism, developed the concept of reification, and argued for totality as the key methodological category of Marxist analysis.

nodes.label: Black Marxism
nodes.description: Cedric Robinson's foundational text introducing the concept of racial capitalism — that capitalism has always been articulated through racial differentiation and the super-exploitation of racialized people.

nodes.label: The Conquest of Bread
nodes.description: Kropotkin's vision of a libertarian communist society. Argues that society should be organized around free communes that provide for human needs without coercion or central authority.

nodes.label: Capitalist Realism
nodes.description: Mark Fisher's analysis of contemporary capitalism and its cultural logic. Coins the term 'capitalist realism' for the widespread belief that capitalism is easier to imagine than any alternative.

nodes.label: The Accumulation of Capital
nodes.description: Luxemburg's major theoretical work. Argues that capitalism requires non-capitalist environments to absorb its surplus production, making imperialism structurally necessary to capital.

nodes.label: Orientalism
nodes.description: Edward Said's foundational postcolonial text. Analyzes how Western scholarship constructed the 'Orient' as inferior and exotic, providing ideological justification for colonialism.

nodes.label: The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction
nodes.description: Benjamin's essay on how mechanical reproduction destroys the 'aura' of the artwork. Analyzes the political implications of mass reproduction for fascist and progressive aesthetics.

nodes.label: Empire
nodes.description: Negri and Hardt's analysis of the new form of global sovereignty that emerged after the Cold War. Argues that nation-states have been superseded by an imperial network form of sovereignty.
